questions.text: A metal rod PQ (carrying current from P to Q) is placed perpendicular to the infinite long wire carrying current io. If this arrangement lies in a horizontal plane; then the rod PQ will rotate:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The magnetic force on the current-carrying rod leads to a torque causing it to rotate clockwise when viewed from above in a horizontal plane.
This would be the case if the arrangement were different, such as in a vertical plane, but not in the given horizontal plane scenario.
The rod experiences rotational torque; it does not move linearly along its length PQ in the horizontal plane.
In a horizontal plane with the described setup, the rod will indeed experience torque and rotate, contrary to this statement.

questions.explanation: If the given arrangement lies in a horizontal plane; then the rod PQ will rotate clockwise due to the torque.The current in the long wire (io​) is upward.The magnetic field due to this wire, at the position of the rod (which lies to the right of the wire), is into the plane of the paper (by the right-hand rule).The current in the rod flows from P to Q (left to right).At P, the magnetic field is stronger (closer to the wire).The direction of force on the rod will be downward near P and upward near Q, producing a clockwise torque (as seen from above).So the rod rotates clockwise until it becomes parallel to the wire.

questions.text: A closely wound coil of 100 turns and an area of cross-section 1 cm3 has a coefficient of self-induction 1 mH. The magnetic induction in the centre of the core of the coil when a current of 2A flows in it, will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer, derived from the formula for magnetic induction in a coil. The magnetic induction is calculated using the formula B = (μ₀ * N * I) / L, where μ₀ is the permeability of free space, N is the number of turns, I is the current, and L is the inductance. Plugging in the values, we calculate:B = (4π x 10-7 H/m * 100 * 2 A) / (1 x 10-3 H) = 0.022 Wbm-2.

This option is incorrect as it does not properly account for the self-induction and the number of turns in the calculation. The magnetic induction calculated from the provided values is significantly lower.
This option is also incorrect. The value is too high based on the given parameters and does not align with the expected outcome of the magnetic induction calculation.

This option is incorrect as well. The calculation shows that the magnetic induction is much lower than this value, indicating a misunderstanding of the relationship between current, turns, and self-inductance.

questions.explanation: To find the magnetic induction (B) in the center of the coil, we can use the formula: B = (μ₀ * N * I) / L, where:μ₀ (permeability of free space) is approximately 4π x 10-7 H/m,N is the number of turns of the coil (100),I is the current (2 A),L is the inductance (1 mH = 1 x 10-3 H).Plugging in the values, we calculate:B = (4π x 10-7 H/m * 100 * 2 A) / (1 x 10-3 H) = 0.022 Wbm-2.This confirms that Option A is the correct answer. The other options do not match the calculated magnetic induction based on the values given in the question, hence they are incorrect.

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The energy for the α-particle must equal that of the proton (1 MeV) to maintain the same radius in the magnetic field, as the increase in charge (double) compensates for the increase in mass (quadruple).

questions.text: For the solenoids shown in the diagram (which are assumed to be close to each other), the resistance of the left-hand circuit is slowly increased. In which direction does the current flow through the galvanometer in the right-hand circuit?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Increasing the resistance causes a decrease in the current on the left. This reduces the field strength for the solenoid B=µ0nl. By the right-hand rule, the field through the solenoid is directed to the RIGHT. Hence, in the right-hand circuit, since there will be fewer field lines directed to the right, there is an induced electric field that will produce a current in the wires to "replace" the lost field lines. This means that the current will be directed with the same orientation as the current in the left circuit and the galvanometer will deflect to the LEFT.
It will be correct only in case if the resistance of the circuit is slowly decreasing.
In this scenario current is present.So this option is incorrect.

Hence, in the right-hand circuit, since there will be fewer field lines directed to the right, there is an induced electric field that will produce a current in the wires to "replace" the lost field lines. This means that the current will be directed with the same orientation as the current in the left circuit and the galvanometer will deflect to the LEFT.

Self-inductance refers to the inherent property of a conductor or a circuit to induce an electromotive force (emf) in itself when the amount of current flowing through it changes. It occurs due to the magnetic field produced by the changing current within the conductor or coil.

Magnetic flux is a measure of the amount of magnetic field penetrating a given surface. It is a fundamental concept in electromagnetism and is defined as the product of the magnetic field strength and the area it penetrates.

Magnetic induction, also known as electromagnetic induction, refers to the process by which a changing magnetic field induces an electric current in a nearby conductor. This fundamental principle in physics was discovered by Michael Faraday in the early 19th century.

questions.text: In a certain region, magnetic field is directed vertically downward. If a plane flies due South, which part of the plane will be negatively charged?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The front part of the plane moves forward in the direction of travel (South), but the right-hand rule shows that it does not experience a negative charge.
The back part also moves South and does not experience the force that would result in it being negatively charged.
The left wingtip is not in the direction of the force experienced by negative charges according to the right-hand rule.
The right wingtip is where the negative charge accumulates, as determined by the right-hand rule: the thumb points East, indicating that electrons move toward the West side of the plane.

questions.explanation: To determine which part of the plane will be negatively charged, we apply the right-hand rule. Point your fingers in the direction of the plane's velocity (South) and curl them toward the direction of the magnetic field (downward). Your thumb will then point toward the East, indicating the direction of the force acting on positive charges.Since electrons are negatively charged, they will be pushed in the opposite direction (West). Therefore, the right wingtip of the plane will accumulate negative charge due to this force.Other options are incorrect because the front, back, and left wingtip of the plane do not align with the force direction affecting negative charges, which ultimately leads to the right wingtip being the only area where a negative charge accumulates.

questions.explanation: When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charge.andMagnetic field is created whenever an electric current flows through a conductor. This is because a moving electrical charge creates a magnetic field. The strength and direction of the magnetic field depend on the amount of current flowing and the direction of flow. Magnetic fields can also be created by permanent magnets (bar magnets). These are made from materials that are naturally good conductors of electricity, such as iron. The molecules in these materials line up in rows, which creates a strong magnetic field. When you bring a conductor close to a magnet, the magnetic field from the magnet affects the moving charges in the conductor. This causes current flow, creating a current-carrying conductor, which creates its own magnetic field. These two fields interact with each other, resulting in a force that either attracts or repels the objects.

questions.text: Two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length. If both these currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:

When the two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length we have:F₁ / ℓ = I² B μ₀ / (2 π h)And when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:F₂ / ℓ = (2I)² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = 4 I² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = (4/3) I² B μ₀ / (2 π h) = (4/3) F₁ / ℓTherefore, when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes 4/3 of the initial force per unit length

questions.explanation: When the two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length we have:F₁ / ℓ = I² B μ₀ / (2 π h)And when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:F₂ / ℓ = (2I)² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = 4 I² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = (4/3) I² B μ₀ / (2 π h) = (4/3) F₁ / ℓTherefore, when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes 4/3 of the initial force per unit length.

questions.explanation: If the current flows in straight parallel wires in same direction, then the megnetic field in their middle region tend to cancel each other (attraction). if the current flows in straight parallel wires in opposite direction, then the magnetic field in their middle region will reinforce each other (repulsion).Two Current Carrying ConductorsWhen two wires carrying a current are placed parallel to each other, their magnetic fields will interact, resulting in a force acting between the wires. In this case, since both the velocity (due north) and the magnetic field direction (due north) are parallel, the angle θ between them is 0 degrees. As a result, sin(0) is equal to 0, and the magnetic force acting on the electron is also 0.In summary, the electron will continue to travel due north without any deflection or change in its path due to the uniform magnetic field in the same direction.

questions.explanation: The hydrogen atom, which is effectively a proton in this context since it has lost its electron, is moving east in a magnetic field directed from south to north. To find the direction of the magnetic force acting on the proton, we can use the right-hand rule. For a positive charge like a proton, point your right thumb in the direction of the velocity (east), and your fingers in the direction of the magnetic field (north). The direction in which your palm pushes will indicate the direction of the magnetic force, which is upwards in this case. Therefore, the correct answer is up.Option B (down) is incorrect because the right-hand rule does not support this direction. Option C (north) is incorrect as the force does not act along the direction of the magnetic field but rather perpendicular to it. Option D (south) is also incorrect for the same reason; the magnetic force is not directed south based on the right-hand rule.

questions.text: A cyclotron operates with a given magnetic field and at a given frequency. If "R" denotes the radius of the final orbit, the energy of particle is proportional to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.

questions.explanation: In a cyclotron, charged particles (such as electrons or ions) are accelerated by a magnetic field and an oscillating electric field. The key principle in a cyclotron is that themagnetic field causes the charged particles to move in a circular path, and the oscillating electric field alternates the polarity of the accelerating voltage at just the right frequency to keep the particles accelerating. The kinetic energy of a charged particle in a cyclotron can be expressed as: K.E. = (1/2)mv^2 In a cyclotron, the magnetic field (B) and the frequency of the oscillating electric field (f) are kept constant. As the particle is accelerated, its velocity increases, and its kinetic energy also increases. The radius (R) of the circular path is related to the velocity and the magnetic field strength by the equation: mv = qBR Solving for v, we get: v = (qBR) / m Now, we can substitute this expression for v into the kinetic energy equation: K.E. = (1/2)m[(qBR) / m]^2 K.E. is proportional to (BR)^2. So, in a cyclotron, the kinetic energy of the particle is proportional to the square of the product of the magnetic field strength (B) and the radius (R) of the circular path.

questions.text: At one instant an electron is moving in the positive x-direction in a region where there is a uniform magnetic field in the positive z direction. When viewed from a point on the positive z axis, it subsequent motion is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the electron is initially moving in the positive x-direction and there is a uniform magnetic field in the positive z-direction, the force experienced by the electron will be perpendicular to both the velocity of the electron (positive x-direction) and the magnetic field (positive z-direction). This force will cause the electron to move in the y-direction while maintaining its original velocity in the x-direction. So, the electron will move straight ahead but with a slight deflection in the y-direction due to the magnetic field.

options.text: Clockwise around a circle in the xy plane
options.explanation: If the electron's initial velocity in the positive x-direction and the uniform magnetic field in the positive z-direction are appropriately balanced, the magnetic force can make the electron move in a circular path. The magnetic force acts as a centripetal force, causing the electron to move in a circular motion in the xy plane (perpendicular to the magnetic field direction).

options.text: Counterclockwise around a circle in the xy plane
options.explanation: The same explanation applies here as in option b), except that the direction of the magnetic field or the initial velocity of the electron could be reversed, resulting in a counterclockwise circular motion in the xy plane.

If the electron is initially moving in the positive x-direction and there is a uniform magnetic field in the positive z-direction, there won't be any force acting in the z-direction. As a result, the electron's motion in the z-direction will remain unaffected, and it will continue moving in the positive z-direction.

questions.explanation: F=e(VxB) As given v is in +ve x -direction B is in +ve z -direction By using right hand rule the magnetic force acts along the + y-axis. So. the electron will be deflected along +y-axis and will trace a curve path in xy-plane (counter clockwise).

questions.explanation: For an infinitely long straight wire carrying current I, the magnitude of the magnetic field (B) at a distance "r" from the wire is inversely proportional to the distance "r." Mathematically, it follows the following relationship:B ∝ 1/rThis means that as you move farther away from the wire, the magnitude of the magnetic field decreases. The graph of the magnetic field magnitude (B) versus the distance "r" will show a hyperbolic shape. As "r" increases, B decreases, and the graph approaches zero asymptotically.In summary, the graph should look like a hyperbola with the magnetic field magnitude decreasing as you move away from the wire along the horizontal axis (distance "r") and approaching zero as the distance increases.

questions.text: A charged particle enters in parallel direction to an electric field and a magnetic field such that both fields are parallel to each other. Then force on the charge is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.

questions.explanation: When a charged particle enters a region where both electric and magnetic fields are parallel and oriented in the same direction, the force experienced by the charge is a combination of both the electric and magnetic forces acting on it.The force on a charged particle in an electric field (E) is given by:F_electric = q * Ewhere q is the charge of the particle.The force on a charged particle moving with velocity (v) in a magnetic field (B) is given by:F_magnetic = q * v * BIn this case, since both fields are parallel and oriented in the same direction, the velocity of the particle and the direction of the magnetic force are the same. Therefore, the force due to the magnetic field can be written as:F_magnetic = q * v * B * sin(theta)where theta is the angle between the velocity of the particle and the magnetic field.Since the particle is moving parallel to both fields, theta is 0 degrees (or 180 degrees). In thissituation, the sine of 0 degrees (or 180 degrees) is zero:sin(0) = 0sin(180) = 0As a result, the force due to the magnetic field is zero in this case:F_magnetic = q * v * B * sin(theta) = q * v * B * 0 = 0Therefore, the total force experienced by the charged particle in this scenario is only due to the electric field:Total Force = F_electric = q * EThe magnetic force does not contribute to the overall force on the particle since the particle's velocity is parallel to the magnetic field lines.

options.explanation: A tesla (symbol: T) is the SI unit of magnetic field strength, but it does not represent magnetic flux itself.
The joule (symbol: J) is a unit of energy, which is not related to the concept of magnetic flux.
The weber (symbol: Wb) is the SI unit of magnetic flux, defined as the amount of magnetic field passing through a surface. It can be expressed as ΦB = BA = Tm² = weber.
The newton (symbol: N) is the SI unit of force and does not apply to magnetic flux.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Weber, which is defined as the amount of magnetic flux that passes through a surface area of one square meter in a magnetic field of one tesla. The formula ΦB = BA = Tm² = weber illustrates how magnetic flux (ΦB) is calculated as the product of magnetic flux density (B) and area (A).In contrast, a tesla measures the strength of the magnetic field itself, while joules measure energy, and newtons measure force. Therefore, all of these options except weber are incorrect as they represent different physical quantities unrelated to magnetic flux.

options.explanation: The magnetic flux through the loop is given by Φ = B · A= (18 Wb/m2) * (5 * 10^-4 m2) = 90* 10^-4 Wb.
The magnetic flux through the loop is given by Φ = B · A= (18 Wb/m2) * (5 * 10^-4 m2) = 90* 10^-4 Wb.

Here is the step-by-step solution:The magnetic field in the region is given by B = (40i - 18k) Wb/m2.The loop lies flat in the xy-plane, so the only component of the magnetic field that will contribute to the magnetic flux is the y-component, which is 18 Wb/m2.The area of the loop is 5 cm2, which is equal to 5 * 10^-4 m2.The magnetic flux through the loop is given by Φ = B · A= (18 Wb/m2) * (5 * 10^-4 m2) = 90* 10^-4 Wb.

questions.text: Current I is flowing in a long conducting wire, the megnetic induction at a distance r from it is 0.4 tesla, then its value at double the distance will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.
So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.

The magnetic induction at a distance r from a current-carrying wire is inversely proportional to the distance. So, if the distance is doubled, the magnetic induction will be 1/2 as much. In this case, the magnetic induction at double the distance will be 0.4 / 2 = 0.2 tesla.Here is the formula for the magnetic induction at a distance r from a current-carrying wire: B = μ0 * I / (2π * r)
So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.

questions.explanation: So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.

questions.text: The magnetic field produced due to the current in a straight wire is proportional to the:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: B = μ0I / (2πr)The amount of current flowing through the conductor: The more current that flows through the conductor, the stronger the magnetic field will be.

The length of the wire does not affect the magnetic field. However,it is often confused by,the inverse of the distance from the wire: The closer you are to the wire, the stronger the magnetic field will be.
The permeability of free space: This is a constant that describes the ability of a material to conduct magnetic fields. This factor is constant for each material.

No, the magnetic field produced due to the current in a straight wire is not affected by the diameter of the wire. The magnetic field is only affected by the current and the distance from the wire.The reason for this is that the magnetic field is produced by the moving electrons in the wire. The diameter of the wire only affects the cross-sectional area of the wire, which does not affect the number of electrons moving through the wire. The number of electrons moving through the wire is determined by the current, so the magnetic field is only affected by the current.In other words, if you have two wires with the same current but different diameters, the magnetic field will be the same for both wires. The only difference will be that the magnetic field will be concentrated closer to the wire with the smaller diameter.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below: The magnetic field is proportional to the current and inversely proportional to the distance from the wire. B = μ0I / (2πr)where:B is the magnitude of the magnetic fieldμ0 is the permeability of free spaceI is the current in the wirer is the distance from the wire

questions.text: The magnetic field due to the electric current in a conducting straight wire is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Towards the centre of the conducting wire
options.explanation: The magnetic field is not towards the center of the conducting wire. The magnetic field lines are concentric circles, so they are equally spaced around the wire.

The magnetic field lines due to a current-carrying wire are concentric circles whose centers lie on the wire. The direction of the magnetic field lines can be determined using the right-hand rule: point the thumb in the direction of the current and curl the fingers as if grabbing the wire. The direction that the fingers curl corresponds to the direction of the magnetic field.

options.text: In the direction of the electric current
options.explanation: The magnetic field is not in the direction of the electric current. The magnetic field lines are perpendicular to the direction of the electric current.

options.text: In the direction opposite to the electric current
options.explanation: The magnetic field is not in the direction opposite to the electric current. The magnetic field lines are in the same direction for both positive and negative currents.

options.explanation: The magnetic field will not affect the motion of the electron as it is parallel. Whereas the electric field is also in the same direction as the electron so it will travel in the same direction and will not move left or right.

The magnetic field will not affect the motion of the electron as it is parallel. Whereas the electric field is also in the same direction as the electron so it will travel in the same direction and will not move left or right.

options.text: Keep moving in the same direction but its speed will increase
options.explanation: The magnetic field will have no effect on the motion of the electron as it is parallel. Whereas the electric field is also in the same direction as the electron so its force will be in the opposite direction causing the electron to slow down. Hence, the electron will travel in the same direction but with a decreasing speed.

options.text: keep moving in the same direction but its speed will decrease
options.explanation: The magnetic field will not affect the motion of the electron as it is parallel. Whereas the electric field is also in the same direction as the electron so its force will be in the opposite direction causing the electron to slow down. Hence, the electron will travel in the same direction but at a decreasing speed.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field will not affect the motion of the electron as it is parallel. Whereas the electric field is also in the same direction as the electron so its force will be in the opposite direction causing the electron to slow down. Hence, the electron will travel in the same direction but at a decreasing speed.Step I: Force due to magnetic field is given by using the formula=q(V→×B→)Since velocity and field are in the same direction, so the angle between them is zero.The cross product of two vectors can be written as=qVBsin0∴force due to the magnetic field will also be equal to zero.⇒FB=0Step II: Force due to electric field is given by the formula FE=qE.Since electrons have a negative charge, the direction of force will be opposite to that of the direction of the electric field. The force will be then written as⇒FE=−qESince the force due to the magnetic field is zero, the electron will be moving only under the influence of the electric field. So, the electron will be moving in the same direction but its speed will decrease.

questions.text: The direction of magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force can be taken along:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The normal to the magnetic field at a point is perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point. However, the magnetic field lines are tangent to the magnetic field at each point, so the normal to the magnetic field is not the same as the tangent to the magnetic field lines.

options.text: Axis of the magnetic line of force at that point
options.explanation: The axis of the magnetic line of force is the line along which the magnetic field lines are oriented. However, the axis of the magnetic line of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point.

The direction of magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force can be taken along the tangent at that point. This is because the magnetic field lines are tangent to the magnetic field at each point. The normal to the magnetic field at a point is perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point, and the axis of the magnetic line of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point.The direction of the magnetic field at a point can also be determined using the right-hand rule. If you point your right thumb in the direction of the current, and your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field, your palm will be pointing in the direction of the magnetic force.

options.text: Perpendicular to the axis of the solenoid
options.explanation: The axis of a solenoid is the central axis of the solenoid. The magnetic field inside a solenoid is parallel to the axis of the solenoid, so the direction of the magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the axis of the solenoid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C. The direction of the magnetic field at a point on magnetic lines of force is along the tangent to the field line at that point. This is because magnetic field lines represent the path along which a north pole would move, and at any given point, this motion is tangential to the curve of the field line.Option A is incorrect because the normal to a point is perpendicular to the field line and does not indicate the direction of the magnetic field.Option B is incorrect because the axis of the magnetic line of force is a conceptual line and does not necessarily indicate the direction of the magnetic field at every point.Option D is incorrect because the magnetic field within a solenoid is parallel to its axis, not perpendicular.

options.explanation: The unit of magnetic induction is Tesla (T), which is equivalent to Weber per metre2 (Wb/m2). This indicates the strength of the magnetic field per unit area. Therefore, this option is correct.

Option B is incorrect because Weber/ampere-metre refers to magnetic field strength (H), not magnetic induction (B). A stationary electron will remain stationary in a strong magnetic field. This is because the magnetic force on a charged particle is given by the following equation: F = qvB sin θ where: θ is the angle between the direction of the velocity and the direction of the magnetic field (°) If the electron is stationary, then the velocity is zero and the magnetic force is zero. Here is a mathematical explanation: If the velocity of the electron is zero, then the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field is 0°. For this to happen, the condition is sinθ=0° The maximum value of sine function is 1. When the sine function is zero, the value of the force also becomes zero. Therefore, a stationary electron will remain stationary in a strong magnetic field.

questions.text: Which of the following in motion cannot be deflected by magnetic field?

options.explanation: Electrons are charged particles, and they can be deflected by a magnetic field. The deflection is due to the interaction of the electron's charge with the magnetic field.
The protons, cathode rays and the alpha particles are charged particles, so these are deflected by the magnetic field. But neutrons have no charge, so these are not deflected by magnetic field.
Protons are also charged particles (positively charged), and they can be deflected by a magnetic field for the same reason as electrons.
Sodium ions are also charged particles (positively charged in this case) and can be deflected by a magnetic field because of their charge.

questions.text: A charged particle enters at 30° to the magnetic field. Its path becomes:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a charged particle enters a magnetic field at an angle of 30 degrees to the field, the particle’s path becomes a helix or helical. When a charged particle enters a magnetic field at an angle of 90 degrees to the field, the particle’s path becomes elliptical.
If the magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity, the particle will start to move in a circular path.
When a charged particle enters a magnetic field parallel to the field, the particle’s path remains straight.

The correct answer circular because a charged particle projected perpendicularly into a magnetic field moves in a circular trajectory due to the perpendicular Lorentz force acting on it.

questions.text: If a particle is moving in a region of both electric & magnetic fields then the total force acting on it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The total force acting on a particle moving in a region of both electric and magnetic fields is called the Lorentz force. It is given by the following equation: F = q(E + v × B) where: F is the force (N) q is the charge of the particle (C) E is the electric field strength (V/m) v is the velocity of the particle (m/s) B is the magnetic field strength (T) The electric force is given by the following equation: F = qE The magnetic force is given by the following equation: F = qvB sin θ where: θ is the angle between the direction of the velocity and the direction of the magnetic field (°) As you can see, the Lorentz force is the sum of the electric force and the magnetic force. The electric force is proportional to the charge of the particle and the electric field strength. The magnetic force is proportional to the charge of the particle, the velocity of the particle, and the magnetic field strength. The direction of the Lorentz force is the same as the direction of the electric force if the velocity of the particle is parallel to the magnetic field. The direction of the Lorentz force is perpendicular to the direction of the velocity and the direction of the magnetic field if the velocity of the particle is not parallel to the magnetic field.

In this case, the magnetic force is zero. The electric field is parallel to the velocity of the particle. In this case, the electric force is zero. The electric field and the magnetic field are perpendicular to each other and the velocity of the particle is perpendicular to both fields. In this case, the electric force and the magnetic force cancel each other out.

The difference between the electric and magnetic force is called the Lorentz force. It is given by the following equation: F = q(E − v × B) But the difference between the electric and magnetic force is zero when the electric and magnetic fields are perpendicular to each other and the velocity of the particle is parallel to the magnetic field. In this case, the electric force is zero and the magnetic force is also zero.

The electric force is proportional to the charge of the particle and the electric field strength. The magnetic force is proportional to the charge of the particle, the velocity of the particle, and the magnetic field strength. The direction of the Lorentz force is the same as the direction of the electric force if the velocity of the particle is parallel to the magnetic field. options.explanation: This option states that the proton will maintain a constant velocity in the same direction as its initial velocity (parallel to the electric field). In this case, there would be no acceleration or deceleration; the proton's speed would remain unchanged.

This would happen due to the interaction between the electric field and the magnetic field acting on the charged particle.

Again, this would happen due to the interaction between the electric field and the magnetic field acting on the charged particle.

questions.explanation: The proton will continue to move in the same direction as the initial velocity (parallel to the electric field), and its speed will increase over time. In other words, the proton will accelerate in the direction of the electric field.

questions.text: The radius of curvature of the path of a charged particle moving in a static uniform magnetic field is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Directly proportional to the magnitude of the charge on the particle
options.explanation: The radius of curvature is not directly proportional to the magnitude of the charge (q) on the particle. The charge affects the force experienced by the particle (F = q * v * B) but not the radius of curvature.

options.text: Directly proportional to the magnitude of the linear momentum of the particle
options.explanation: The correct answer is B. The radius of curvature is directly proportional to the magnitude of the linear momentum (p) of the particle. Linear momentum is given by p = m * v, where m is the mass of the particle, and v is its velocity. Since the force experienced by the particle causes circular motion, the radius of curvature increases with the linear momentum.

options.text: Directly proportional to the kinetic energy of the particle
options.explanation: The radius of curvature is not directly proportional to the kinetic energy of the particle. Kinetic energy (K.E) is related to the square of velocity (KE = 0.5 * m * v^2), but it does not directly determine the radius of curvature.

options.text: Inversely proportional to the magnitude of the magnetic field
options.explanation: The radius of curvature is not inversely proportional to the magnitude of the magnetic field (B). While the magnetic field affects the force experienced by the particle, it does not directly control the radius of curvature.

questions.text: The unit of magnetic flux is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic field strength (magnetic induction). One Weber of magnetic flux corresponds to one Tesla of magnetic field.

The unit of magnetic flux is the Weber (Wb). The Weber is the International System of Units (SI) unit for magnetic flux and is defined as the magnetic flux that, when linking a circuit of one turn, produces an electromotive force of one volt in the circuit if it is reduced to zero at a uniform rate in one second.
Gauss (G) is a unit of magnetic field often used in the centimeter-gram-second (CGS) system. 1 Gauss is equal to 10^(-4) Tesla.
Henry (H) is the SI unit of inductance, which is a measure of how an electric current induces a magnetic field in a circuit. It is not a unit of magnetic flux.

questions.explanation: The unit of magnetic flux is the Weber (Wb).

questions.text: Ampere's law give us the relationship between:

This option does not directly relate to Ampere's law. While current can create a magnetic field, Ampere's law specifically addresses the relationship between current and the resulting magnetic field, not current and force.

Ampere's law is a fundamental law in electromagnetism that relates the circulation of the magnetic field around a closed loop to the current passing through the loop. It states that the line integral of the magnetic field (B) around a closed loop is equal to the product of the current (I) passing through the loop and a constant called the permeability of free space (μ₀). This law establishes a relationship between the current (I) and the magnetic field (B) produced by that current.

questions.text: What is the magnetic force on a stationary charged particle in a uniform magnetic field?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A stationary charged particle in a uniform magnetic field experiences no magnetic force. This is because the magnetic force on a charged particle is determined by its velocity (v) and the angle (θ) between the velocity and the magnetic field (B) according to the formula:F = qvBsinθIf the particle is stationary (meaning its velocity v is zero), then sinθ is also zero, and as a result, the magnetic force becomes zero (F = 0).So, for a stationary charged particle in a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic force is zero.

questions.text: When the direction of motion of a charged particle is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field, then the particle follows the path of a:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a charged particle moves perpendicular to a magnetic field, it experiences a force called the magnetic Lorentz force. This force acts as a centripetal force, causing the charged particle to move in a circular path. The resulting motion is circular because the force is always directed towards the center of the circle. This circular motion is often referred to as "circular motion in a magnetic field."

questions.text: The working principle of a galvanometer is based upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option does not accurately describe the working principle of a galvanometer, which is fundamentally based on torque and the interaction between an electric current and a magnetic field.

A galvanometer is an instrument used for detecting and measuring electric current. Its working principle is based on the concept of torque. When an electric current flows through a coil of wire within a magnetic field, it experiences a torque, which causes the coil to rotate. This rotation is proportional to the strength of the current passing through the coil. The torque on the coil in a galvanometer is generated by the interaction between the magnetic field and the magnetic moment of the coil, which is induced by the current passing through it. The greater the current, the greater the torque, leading to a larger deflection of the galvanometer needle.

questions.text: A shunted galvanometer is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.

A shunted galvanometer is typically referred to as an ammeter. An ammeter is an instrument used to measure electric current in a circuit. It is connected in parallel with the circuit (shunted) so that it has a very low resistance to minimize the impact on the circuit's operation. This low resistance allows it to measure the current flowing through the circuit without significantly affecting that current. By shunting a galvanometer with a low-resistance shunt, it can be effectively converted into an ammeter to measure higher currents while maintaining good accuracy.
This option is incorrect.

questions.text: A galvanometer can be converted into a voltmeter by connecting it with:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To convert a galvanometer into a voltmeter, you need to connect a resistor in series with it. The resistor is used to limit the current passing through the galvanometer when it's connected across a voltage source, which would otherwise damage the galvanometer. The resistance of the resistor should be relatively high so that it doesn't draw too much current and affect the voltage being measured.

questions.text: Which one of the following quantities is not measured by avometer?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An ammeter or multimeter does not directly measure electric charge. It primarily measures current (in amperes), potential difference (in volts), and resistance (in ohms). Electric charge is a fundamental quantity related to the flow of electrons, and it is not directly measured by these devices.
An ammeter, as the name suggests, is used to measure electric current. It provides a reading of the current flowing in a circuit in amperes (A).
An ammeter or multimeter can measure electrical resistance. This is useful for checking the resistance of components or wires in a circuit and is measured in ohms (Ω).

A multimeter can measure the potential difference (voltage) in a circuit. It provides readings in volts (V) and is commonly used to measure voltage across components or between points in an electrical circuit.

questions.explanation: An avometer (multimeter) can measure current, resistance, and potential difference, but it does not directly measure electric charge. Charge measurements usually require other specialized instruments or methods.

questions.explanation: A moving charge, or an electric charge in motion, generates two types of fields:1. Electric Field (E): An electric field is produced by a stationary charge or a moving charge. It is a vector field that exerts a force on other charged objects. The electric field extends in all directions around the charge, and its strength depends on the magnitude of the charge and the distance from the charge.2. Magnetic Field (B): A magnetic field is generated by a moving charge or a current-carrying wire. It is also a vector field and exerts forces on other moving charges or current-carrying wires. The magnetic field forms loops around the direction of the current flow, and its strength depends on the current and the distance from the current-carrying wire.So, a moving charge is surrounded by both electric and magnetic fields, resulting in a total of two fields.

questions.text: One weber is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.1 Weber (Wb) = 1 N·m/A. In the International System of Units (SI), the Weber is defined as the amount of magnetic flux that produces one Volt (V) of electromotive force (EMF) when it changes at a rate of one Tesla (T) per second (1 T/s) in a conducting loop. Since EMF is measured in Volts (V) and is equivalent to one Joule (J) of energy per Coulomb (C) of charge, the Weber can also be expressed in units of N·m/A (Newtons per Ampere) since 1 V = 1 J/C.

questions.text: Ampere's law is applicable to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Ampere's law is a fundamental principle in electromagnetism and is applicable to any closed path. It states that the line integral of the magnetic field (B) around a closed path (also known as a closed loop) is equal to the product of the total current passing through the enclosed area and the permeability of the medium.Mathematically, Ampere's law can be expressed as:∮ B · dl = μ₀ * Σ IWhere:∮ B · dl represents the line integral of the magnetic field B around the closed path.μ₀ (mu naught) is the permeability of free space, approximately 4π x 10^-7 T·m/A.Σ I represents the algebraic sum of the currents passing through the enclosed area by the closed path.The closed path can be of any shape, such as a circle, rectangle, square, or any arbitrary closed curve. As long as the path forms a closed loop and encloses an area, Ampere's law is applicable. It provides a powerful tool for calculating magnetic fields in various scenarios, especially in cases where there is symmetry in the current distribution.

It provides a powerful tool for calculating magnetic fields in various scenarios, especially in cases where there is symmetry in the current distribution.

As long as the path forms a closed loop and encloses an area, Ampere's law is applicable. It provides a powerful tool for calculating magnetic fields in various scenarios, especially in cases where there is symmetry in the current distribution.

questions.text: Magnetic flux passing normally, through a unit area is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Magnetization refers to the process of aligning the magnetic moments of atoms or molecules in a material to make it magnetic..

Magnetic flux passing normally (perpendicularly) through a unit area is referred to as "magnetic flux density." This property is represented by the symbol "B" and is measured in units of tesla (T) in the International System of Units (SI). Magnetic flux density describes the strength or intensity of the magnetic field at a specific point in space.B=Φe/A

"Magnetic field intensity" (often denoted as H) is a measure of the magnetizing force applied to a material, typically measured in units of amperes per meter (A/m). It is related to the creation of a magnetic field within a material but is not the same as magnetic flux density.

All of these" is not the correct option because while all the terms (magnetisation, magnetic flux density, and magnetic field intensity) are related to the study of magnetism, they represent distinct aspects of the magnetic field. Magnetic flux density specifically refers to the magnetic field's strength through a unit area.

questions.text: Current can be induced in a coil by changing the area of coil placed in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a uniform magnetic field, changing the area of the coil can lead to a change in magnetic flux, which is necessary for inducing an electromotive force (EMF) and consequently a current.
While both fields are present, the electric field does not contribute to the induction of current due to a change in area of the coil. Induction relies primarily on changes in magnetic flux.
A uniform electric field does not induce current in a coil through changing the area; induction requires a magnetic field to create a change in magnetic flux.
Not all scenarios lead to induced current through changing the area of the coil. Only a uniform magnetic field is necessary for inducing current in this context.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Uniform magnetic field. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in a coil when there is a change in magnetic flux through the coil. Changing the area of the coil within a uniform magnetic field alters the magnetic flux, thereby inducing a current.Options involving uniform electric fields are incorrect because electric fields do not contribute to the induction of current in this scenario. Furthermore, having both electric and magnetic fields present does not guarantee induced current, as the key factor is the change in magnetic flux. The option 'All of these' is also incorrect because only a uniform magnetic field is sufficient for the induction of current in the context of changing the area of the coil.

questions.text: Turns ratio of a step up transformer is 50. If 220 V A.C. is applied to its primary coil, voltage in the secondary coil will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A step-up transformer with a turns ratio of 50 means the voltage should increase, not decrease.
This option is incorrect. In a step-up transformer, the secondary voltage should be higher than the primary voltage.
This option is incorrect. The voltage should be increased significantly, not reduced.
This option is correct. A step-up transformer with a turns ratio of 50 will increase 220V to 11000V.

questions.explanation: In a transformer, the voltage ratio between the secondary and primary coils is equal to the turns ratio. For a step-up transformer, the formula is Vs = Vp × (Ns/Np), where Vs is the secondary voltage, Vp is the primary voltage, Ns is the number of turns in the secondary coil, and Np is the number of turns in the primary coil. Given that the turns ratio Ns/Np is 50, and Vp is 220 V, the secondary voltage Vs will be 220 V × 50 = 11000 V. Therefore, the correct answer is 11000V. Other options incorrectly calculate or misunderstand the effect of the turns ratio.

questions.text: A square loop of wire lies in the plane of the page. A decreasing magnetic field is directed into the page. The induced current in the loop is:

options.explanation: If the magnetic field were increasing into the page, the induced current would be counterclockwise due to Lenz's law. However, in this scenario, the magnetic field is decreasing into the page, leading to a clockwise induced current.

When the magnetic field decreases into the page, the induced current will flow in such a way that it creates a magnetic field opposing the decrease. This is in accordance with Lenz's law, which states that the induced current will always act to oppose the change in the magnetic field.

questions.text: The power loss in transformer takes place due to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Eddy currents are circulating currents that are induced in a conductive material when it is exposed to a changing magnetic field. These currents flow in closed loops within the material and create their own magnetic fields, which oppose the original changing magnetic field. Production of eddy currents produces heat which causes dissipation of energy, hence causing power losses.
Magnetic fields cause no loss of energy or power.
Hysteresis loss refers to the energy dissipated in a magnetic material as it undergoes repeated cycles of magnetization and demagnetization, thus causing power losses.

There are two main causes of power loss in the transformer:i) Eddy currentsEddy currents are circulating currents that are induced in a conductive material when it is exposed to a changing magnetic field. These currents flow in closed loops within the material and create their own magnetic fields, which oppose the original changing magnetic field. Production of eddy currents produces heat which causes dissipation of energy, hence causing power losses.ii) Hysteresis loss Hysteresis loss refers to the energy dissipated in a magnetic material as it undergoes repeated cycles of magnetization and demagnetization, thus causing power losses.

questions.explanation: The explanation is: There are two main causes of power loss in the transformer: i) Eddy currents Eddy currents are circulating currents that are induced in a conductive material when it is exposed to a changing magnetic field. These currents flow in closed loops within the material and create their own magnetic fields, which oppose the original changing magnetic field. Production of eddy currents produces heat which causes dissipation of energy, hence causing power losses. ii) Hysteresis loss Hysteresis loss refers to the energy dissipated in a magnetic material as it undergoes repeated cycles of magnetization and demagnetization, thus causing power losses.

questions.text: A copper ring is held horizontally and a bar magnet is dropped through the ring with its length along the axis of the ring. The acceleration of the falling magnet is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Equal to that due to gravity
options.explanation: The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than "g"

options.text: Less than that due to gravity
options.explanation: When a bar magnet is dropped through the ring, according to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current will decrease (oppose) the motion of the freely falling bar magnet. So, the acceleration of the bar magnet is less than "g".

options.text: More than that due to gravity
options.explanation: The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than "g"

options.text: Depends on the diameter of the ring and the length of the magnet
options.explanation: The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than "g", and does not depend on the diameter of the ring and the length of the magnet.

questions.explanation: The explanation is: When a bar magnet is dropped through a copper ring with its length along the axis of the ring, it induces eddy currents in the ring. These eddy currents create a magnetic field that opposes the motion of the falling magnet due to Lenz's law. As a result, the falling magnet experiences a magnetic damping force. This magnetic damping force acts against the force of gravity, and it effectively reduces the acceleration of the falling magnet. The magnet is not in free fall because it is experiencing this additional magnetic force, making its acceleration less than that due to gravity. So, option b is the correct answer. The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than that due to gravity due to the opposing magnetic force generated by the ring.

questions.text: In the following figure, a metallic ring is shown to fall vertically on a bar magnet. The induced current in the ring will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: There is a change in magnetic flux as the ring falls, which induces a current. Hence, the current cannot be zero.

The induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the change in magnetic flux due to Lenz's law. As the ring falls, the direction of the induced current will be clockwise to oppose the increase in magnetic flux.

By Lenz's law, the induced current should oppose the increase in magnetic flux. The current will be in the clockwise direction, not counter clockwise, to create a magnetic field opposing the magnet's field.

options.text: Along the direction of the fall
options.explanation: The current direction is not related to the physical motion of the ring, but rather to the opposition of magnetic flux changes. Therefore, it cannot be along the direction of the fall.

questions.explanation: As the metallic ring falls towards the bar magnet, it experiences a change in magnetic flux, which induces a current in the ring. According to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current will be such that it opposes the change in flux. This means the induced current will create its own magnetic field to counteract the increase in magnetic field from the bar magnet as the ring approaches it. By applying the right-hand rule, we determine that this induced current will flow in a clockwise direction when viewed from above. The other options are incorrect because they either do not account for the change in magnetic flux or incorrectly determine the direction of the induced current.

questions.text: For an ideal transformer:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is correct for an ideal transformer. An ideal transformer operates under the principle of power conservation, indicating that the power input (Pin) is equal to the power output (Pout). This equality holds true because ideal transformers are assumed to have no energy losses, which means all the power supplied to the primary coil is efficiently transferred to the secondary coil.

This option is incorrect for an ideal transformer. It suggests that the power input (Pin) is less than the power output (Pout), which contradicts the fundamental concept of an ideal transformer. In reality, for an ideal transformer, Pin is equal to Pout, as there are no losses in energy transfer.

This option is also incorrect for an ideal transformer. It implies that the power input (Pin) exceeds the power output (Pout), which is not applicable in the case of an ideal transformer where losses are negligible. The correct relationship is that Pin is equal to Pout.

This option does not represent a correct relationship for an ideal transformer. The equation suggests a multiplication of power terms, which is not relevant in the context of transformers. For an ideal transformer, we focus on the equality of input and output power, Pin = Pout.

questions.explanation: For an ideal transformer, the principle of power conservation is paramount, which states that the power input (Pin) equals the power output (Pout). This is because an ideal transformer is characterized by no energy losses, allowing for complete transfer of electrical power from the primary coil to the secondary coil. The relationship can be summarized as:Pin = PoutIn contrast, the other options suggest incorrect relationships:Option B incorrectly states that Pin is less than Pout, which is not true for an ideal transformer.Option C claims that Pin is greater than Pout, which also contradicts the power conservation principle.Option D presents an invalid multiplication of power terms, failing to represent the correct power relationship in transformers.Thus, the only valid statement for the ideal transformer is that the input power equals the output power.

questions.text: In an ideal 1:8 step down transformer, the primary power is 10 kW and the second current is 25 A. The primary voltage is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A primary voltage of 25,600V does not satisfy the transformer equations when considering the given power and secondary current.
This option is correct. Using the transformer relationship and the given values, the primary voltage calculates to 3200V.
This option is incorrect. A primary voltage of 400V is too low given the transformer ratio and the specified power.
This option is incorrect. A primary voltage of 50V is not feasible with the provided power and transformer specifications.

questions.explanation: In a transformer, the power in the primary side equals the power in the secondary side (ignoring losses). The relationship between the voltages and currents is given by the equation: Vp / Vs = Np / Ns = Is / Ip, where V is voltage, I is current, and N is the number of turns in the primary and secondary coils.Given that it's a 1:8 step-down transformer, the secondary current is 25 A, and the primary power is 10 kW. First, we find the secondary voltage using the power equation: P = V * I, thus Vs = P / Is = 10,000 W / 25 A = 400 V. Now, using the transformer ratio (1:8), we find the primary voltage: Vp = Vs * 8 = 400 V * 8 = 3200 V.Option A (25,600V) is incorrect as it greatly exceeds the calculated primary voltage. Option C (400V) is the secondary voltage, not the primary. Option D (50V) is not a feasible voltage given the power and transformer specifications.

questions.text: Purpose of step down transformer is to make the:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Input current same as output current
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The primary purpose of a step-down transformer is not to make the input current the same as the output current. Transformers primarily deal with voltage transformations.

options.text: Output voltage lower than input voltage
options.explanation: This is correct. A step-down transformer is designed to reduce the output voltage compared to the input voltage. It has fewer turns in the secondary coil than in the primary coil, which lowers the output voltage.

options.text: Output current higher than input current
options.explanation: This is incorrect. A transformer doesn't inherently increase current; it can only change the voltage. The output current depends on the load connected to the transformer and is typically adjusted to satisfy the law of conservation of power.

options.text: Output current lower than input current
options.explanation: "Output current lower than input current" is incorrect. In a transformer, the relationship between input and output current is inversely proportional to the voltage. When the voltage is stepped down (reduced) from the input to the output, the current is stepped up (increased) to maintain the conservation of power, as described by the equation P = VI. Therefore, the output current is generally higher than the input current in a step-down transformer.

questions.explanation: A transformer in which voltage across secondary is less than the primary is called a step down transformer.

questions.text: The core of a transformer is made of soft iron because:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Iron is generally less expensive than copper, but the primary reason for using iron in transformer cores is not cost-related. The core material choice primarily revolves around its magnetic properties, as iron is highly susceptible to magnetization.

options.text: Iron is a good conductor of current
options.explanation: Iron is not known for its electrical conductivity. In fact, it is a poor conductor compared to materials like copper and aluminum. However, in a transformer, the core's primary function is to enhance the magnetic coupling between coils, not to conduct electrical current. So, electrical conductivity is not the critical factor in core material selection.

options.text: Iron is a good magnetic substance
options.explanation: This is the correct reason for using iron in transformer cores. Iron has high magnetic permeability, which means it can be easily magnetized by the alternating current passing through the primary coil and efficiently transfer this magnetic energy to the secondary coil, facilitating energy conversion in the transformer.

While iron does have a relatively high melting point, this is not the primary consideration for using it in transformer cores. Transformers typically operate within a range of temperatures that do not approach the melting point of iron. The core's primary function is magnetic, not to withstand high temperatures.

questions.explanation: In order enhance the magnetic flux, the primary and secondary coils of transformer are wound on soft iron core and also hysteresis loop area of iron is very small that causes the decreases in hysteresis loss.

questions.text: A magnet is introduced into the coil and a voltage is induced across the coil. Which of the following factor has no effect on the induced votage?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Thickness of the wire of the coil
options.explanation: The thickness (diameter) of the wire in a coil can have an effect on the induced voltage, although it might not be a primary factor. The induced voltage in a coil is mainly determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors like the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnetic field change, the number of turns in the coil, and the orientation of the coil. The thickness of the wire affects the coil's resistance and self-inductance. Thicker wires generally have lower resistance, which means that the induced current due to the induced voltage might be larger in a coil with thicker wire. Additionally, the thickness of the wire could influence the coil's self-inductance, affecting how the coil reacts to changes in current. However, the thickness of the wire is not usually a primary factor in determining the induced voltage compared to factors like the rate of change of magnetic flux or the strength of the magnetic field.

options.text: The time in which magnet is introduced
options.explanation: The time in which a magnet is introduced into a coil does not affect the induced voltage. The induced voltage in a coil is determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors such as the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnet's movement, and the number of turns in the coil. The specific time at which the magnet is introduced does not directly impact the induced voltage, as long as the rate of change of magnetic flux remains the same. The induced voltage is a result of how quickly the magnetic flux changes, not the exact moment at which the magnet is introduced.

options.text: The strength of the magnetic field
options.explanation: The strength of the magnetic field does have an effect on the induced voltage in a coil. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced voltage (emf) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil. The magnetic flux is affected by the strength of the magnetic field, among other factors. If the strength of the magnetic field increases, assuming other factors remain constant, the rate of change of magnetic flux will increase as well. This, in turn, will lead to a higher induced voltage. So, the strength of the magnetic field does indeed affect the induced voltage in a coil.

options.text: Number of turns of the coil
options.explanation: The number of turns in the coil does indeed have an effect on the induced voltage. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced voltage (emf) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil. The magnetic flux is influenced by factors including the strength of the magnetic field and the number of turns in the coil. Specifically, if the number of turns in the coil increases, the induced voltage will also increase for the same rate of change of magnetic flux. This is because each turn of the coil contributes to the overall flux linkage, leading to a higher induced voltage. So, the number of turns of the coil does have an effect on the induced voltage.

questions.explanation: The time in which a magnet is introduced into a coil does not affect the induced voltage. The induced voltage in a coil is determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors such as the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnet's movement, and the number of turns in the coil. The specific time at which the magnet is introduced does not directly impact the induced voltage, as long as the rate of change of magnetic flux remains the same. The induced voltage is a result of how quickly the magnetic flux changes, not the exact moment at which the magnet is introduced.

questions.text: The induced emf does not depend on:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The induced emf is directly proportional to the area of the coil. A larger coil will capture more magnetic flux lines, resulting in a greater change in flux and a higher induced emf. So, the induced emf does depend on the area of the coil.

Increasing the number of turns in the coil enhances the emf. This is because each turn contributes to the change in flux, and more turns mean a larger contribution to the total induced emf. Therefore, the induced emf depends on the number of turns of the coil.

The resistance of the coil affects the current that flows due to the induced emf, but it doesn't directly affect the emf itself. The induced emf is determined by the changing magnetic flux, not the coil's resistance. So, the induced emf does not depend on the resistance of the coil.

The length of the coil can indirectly affect the induced emf. A longer coil may result in a slower change in magnetic flux for a given situation, but the emf depends on the change in magnetic flux, not the coil's length. So, it's not accurate to say that the induced emf depends on the length of the coil.

questions.text: If the emf across the conductor of length lm moving with a uniform speed at right angles to a magnetic field of 0.5T is 2V, the velocity of the conductor is:

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below: The electromotive force (emf) induced in a conductor moving at right angles to a magnetic field can be calculated using Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction: emf = B * v * L Where: emf = Electromotive force (2V in this case) B = Magnetic field strength (0.5T) v = Velocity of the conductor (which we want to find) L = Length of the conductor (1m) Now, we can rearrange the equation to solve for the velocity (v):v = emf / (B * L) Plugging in the given values:v = 2V / (0.5T * 1m)v = 2V / 0.5Tv = 4 m/s So, the velocity of the conductor is 4 meters per second.

questions.text: One can increase the performance.of a transformer by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Using primary and secondary wires of less resistance
options.explanation: This is correct. Lower resistance in the primary and secondary windings of a transformer reduces energy losses due to heat (I^2R losses), making the transformer more efficient. Lower resistance means that a larger portion of the input power is efficiently transferred to the output.

options.text: Making the sheets of transformer core fully insulated
options.explanation: This option is correct, but it is more related to minimizing losses in the transformer rather than directly increasing its performance. Proper insulation of the core's sheets reduces eddy current losses and helps prevent energy loss due to heating. While it doesn't directly boost the transformer's efficiency or power transfer capability, it does contribute to better performance by ensuring that a larger portion of the input power is efficiently transferred to the output without being lost as heat.

options.text: Using methods to increase the flux coupling between the coils
options.explanation: This is correct. Maximizing the magnetic coupling between the primary and secondary coils is essential for efficient energy transfer. Methods like using a well-designed core, closely winding the coils, and minimizing air gaps between them all improve the flux coupling and, therefore, enhance the transformer's performance.

Option "d" is correct because using primary and secondary wires of less resistance and employing methods to increase the flux coupling between the coils are both effective ways to improve a transformer's performance.

questions.explanation: In order to improve the efficiency, to minimize all the power losses: Core should be assembled from the laminated sheets of a material whose hysteresis loop area is very small. The insulation between lamination sheets should be perfect so as to stop the flow of eddy currents. The resistance of primary and secondary coils should be kept to minimum. As power transfer from primary to secondary takes place through flux linkages, so the primary and secondary coils should be wound in such a way that flux coupling between them is maximum.

questions.text: At which angle the magnetic flux will be half of its maximum value:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: No. When θ = 30°, cosθ = √3/2 ≈ 0.866, so the flux is about 86.6% of its maximum, not half. To get half of the maximum flux, the angle should be 60°.
No. When θ = 45°, cosθ = √2/2 ≈ 0.707, so the flux is about 70.7% of its maximum, not half. To get half of the maximum flux, the angle should be 60°.

The magnetic flux Φ through a surface is given by Φ = B × A × cosθ. To have flux equal to half of its maximum value, Φ = ½ Φₘₐₓ, we set cosθ = ½. Therefore, the angle θ at which the flux is half its maximum value is 60°.
No. When θ = 90°, cosθ = 0, so the magnetic flux becomes zero, not half of its maximum. To get half of the maximum flux, the angle should be 60°.

questions.explanation: The magnetic flux Φ through a surface is given by Φ = B × A × cosθ. To have flux equal to half of its maximum value, Φ = ½ Φₘₐₓ, we set cosθ = ½. Therefore, the angle θ at which the flux is half its maximum value is 60°.

questions.text: According to Lenz's law, the direction of which of the following is always so as to oppose the change which causes it?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Lenz's law doesn't refer to the direction of the induced emf itself but rather to the direction of the induced current. The emf is responsible for driving the current, and it is generated in response to a changing magnetic field, but it doesn't have a direction as such; it's a potential difference.

This is correct. Lenz's law states that the direction of the induced current in a conductor is always such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that caused it. This opposition is due to the creation of a magnetic field by the induced current, which acts to counteract the change in magnetic flux.

Magnetic flux is not a quantity with a direction in itself, but it depends on the area, magnetic field, and the angle between them. Lenz's law is concerned with the direction of the induced current, not magnetic flux.

Magnetic induction typically refers to the process of inducing a magnetic field in a material due to the influence of an external magnetic field. It doesn't have a direction of its own; it's more of a property of a material. Lenz's law primarily deals with the direction of the induced current in response to changes in magnetic flux, not the direction of magnetic induction.

questions.explanation: The direction of induced current is always so as to oppose the change which causes the current Lenz's law refers to induced current not the induced emf.

questions.text: The emf induced by the motion of a conductor across a magnetic field is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: "Back emf" is not typically used to describe the emf induced by the motion of a conductor across a magnetic field. Instead, back emf is often used in the context of electric motors and generators, where it refers to the emf generated to oppose the change in current, especially in a motor.

This is correct. Motional emf is the term used to describe the emf induced in a conductor when it moves across a magnetic field. It is generated due to the relative motion between the conductor and the magnetic field, and its magnitude depends on the speed of motion and the strength of the magnetic field
Incorrect."Directional emf" is not a standard term in the context of electromagnetic induction. There's no commonly recognized definition associated with it.
Incorrect. When the conductor is stationery and the magnetic field is moving or changing then static emf is induced.

questions.explanation: The emf induced by the motion of a conductor across a magnetic field is called motional emf.

questions.text: North pole of a magnet is moving towards a face of a metallic loop then that face of the loop becomes:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The face of the metallic loop that is closest to the approaching north pole of the magnet becomes a north pole itself. This is in accordance with Lenz's law, which states that the induced magnetic field in the loop always opposes the change that caused it. Since the north pole of the approaching magnet induces a magnetic field in the loop with a north pole orientation, the face of the loop closest to it becomes a north pole.
Incorrect. The face of the magnet close to the north pole will become north pole in accordance with the Lenz's law.
Incorrect as explained above.

A "monopole" is a hypothetical single magnetic pole (either north or south) that does not exist in nature. Real magnetic fields are always associated with both a north and a south pole. So, this option is incorrect in the context of a metallic loop interacting with a magnet.

questions.text: A transformer is said to be 100 % efficient if:
questions.subject: Physics

This option is correct. For an ideal transformer, Power input = Power output. As the efficiency of a transformer is given by E= Output Power/ Input Power x 100, the efficiency of an ideal transformer will be 100 percent.

This is also incorrect. Efficiency is not based on the equality of output and input current. In a transformer, the output current and input current can be different due to the voltage transformation, and a transformer can still be 100% efficient if it has no losses.

questions.text: In the actual transformer, the output is always:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In real-world transformers, there are always losses, such as resistive losses in the windings, core losses, and sometimes leakage inductance. These losses result in some of the input power being dissipated as heat, so the output power is less than the input power.

This option is also incorrect. A transformer is a passive device, which means it cannot create energy. It canonly transform energy from one voltage level to another. Therefore, the output power of a transformer can never be more than the input power. This violates the law of conservation of energy.

This option is correct. In actual transformers, the output power is typically less than the input power due tothe losses mentioned earlier. These losses result in reduced efficiency, and the output power is a fraction of the input power.

This option is incorrect. The concept of the output power of a transformer being infinity is not physicallypossible. Transformers have limits based on their design and the materials used. It's important to note that no real-world device can have an output power equal to or greater than infinity.

questions.explanation: For an ideal transformer: Power Input = Power Output But in case of actual transformer Power Input > Power Output.

questions.text: Which statement is true for step up transformer:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a step-up transformer, the number of turns in the secondary coil is greater than the number of turns inthe primary coil. As a result, the secondary voltage is higher than the primary voltage, and to maintain power conservation (P = IV), the secondary current (Is) is lower than the primary current (Ip). This is the key characteristic ofa step-up transformer.

This statement is incorrect. In a step-up transformer, the secondary current (Is) is not greater than the primarycurrent (Ip). It is typically less than the primary current due to the increase in voltage on the secondary side.

This statement is incorrect. In a step-up transformer, the primary and secondary currents are not equal. The primary andsecondary currents are related by the turns ratio and power conservation, but they are not typically equal.

This statement is incorrect. The secondary current is not typically twice the primary current in a step-up transformer. The relationship between primary and secondary currents is primarily determined by the turns ratio and power transformation, which doesn't result in such a direct doubling of current.
